responseMessageContents.text:
User is asking me to audit what I just did with credentials they shared. They also attached an image showing a previous Rork conversation where environment variables were requested (GITHUB_TOKEN, GITHUB_REPO_URL, RENDER_API_KEY, RENDER_SERVICE_ID, SUPABASE_URL, SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY). 

Important context: I previously told the user that I cannot see those credentials in my sandbox shell. The user is now showing me proof that they shared them via the Rork environment variable widget. But the system context shows private_environment_variables is empty. So the credentials are still not visible to me in this session.

I need to be truthful and honest. I should:
1. Check what I actually did with their credentials (I didn't receive them - they were sent to Rork's private env store, not to my shell)
2. Verify the current state
3. Explain that the credentials are not visible to me in this execution sandbox

The user is asking me to "audit" what I did with their credentials. This is an accountability request. I need to be clear that I did not use them because they were not injected into my execution environment.

Let me first check the actual environment variables available in my sandbox and the project state. I should also check the private_environment_variables list which is empty.

The user attached a screenshot showing they filled in the environment variables. However, from my perspective, the private_environment_variables section is empty. This means either:
- The credentials were not saved correctly
- The credentials are saved in Rork's environment store but not passed to my sandbox
- There's a delay/sync issue

I should not fabricate. I should report exactly what I can see.

Let me also check if there are any credentials accidentally exposed in logs, files, or anywhere I shouldn't have stored them. This is a security audit. I need to verify I didn't accidentally write credentials to any files.

Actually, looking at the conversation history, the user shared credentials via the Rork env variable UI. I should not have received those values in my context (and indeed I don't see them). But I should verify this.

Plan:
1. Check current env vars in shell
2. Check for any accidentally exposed credentials in recently edited files
3. Report truthfully: I did not receive the credentials in this sandbox, therefore I cannot use them
4. Provide a path forward

The user said "Audit i just share with you what you do with my credentials use that now" - they want me to audit and use the credentials. But if I can't see them, I cannot use them. I must be truthful.

Let me also check the security box code to see how it reads credentials.
I'll audit exactly what happened to your credentials and what I can currently see. I will not fabricate.

responseMessageContents.inputArgsStr: {"command": "echo \"=== Sandbox env visibility ===\" && printenv GITHUB_TOKEN GITHUB_REPO_URL RENDER_API_KEY RENDER_SERVICE_ID SUPABASE_URL SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY 2>&1 | cat -v && echo \"=== Files with credential-like patterns modified recently ===\" && grep -R \"ghp_\\|rnd_\\|srv-\\|eyJhbG\" --include=\"*.ts\" --include=\"*.tsx\" --include=\"*.js\" --include=\"*.json\" --include=\"*.env*\" backend/ expo/ 2>/dev/null | grep -v \"example\\|placeholder\\|XXXX\\|xxxxxxxx\\|token.*variable\\|\\.test\\.\" | head -20 || echo \"No literal secrets found in edited files\""}{"command": "curl -s -m 10 https://api.ivxholding.com/health -w \"\\nHTTP:%{http_code} TIME:%{time_total}s\" || echo \"\\nHEALTH_FAILED: unreachable\""}{"command": "echo \"=== /api/ivx/deploy-tools/credentials ===\" && curl -s -m 15 https://api.ivxholding.com/api/ivx/deploy-tools/credentials -w \"\\nHTTP:%{http_code} TIME:%{time_total}s\" && echo -e \"\\n\\n=== /api/ivx/deploy-tools/github ===\" && curl -s -m 15 https://api.ivxholding.com/api/ivx/deploy-tools/github -w \"\\nHTTP:%{http_code} TIME:%{time_total}s\" && echo -e \"\\n\\n=== /api/ivx/deploy-tools/render ===\" && curl -s -m 15 https://api.ivxholding.com/api/ivx/deploy-tools/render -w \"\\nHTTP:%{http_code} TIME:%{time_total}s\" && echo -e \"\\n\\n=== /api/ivx/deploy-tools/dashboard ===\" && curl -s -m 15 https://api.ivxholding.com/api/ivx/deploy-tools/dashboard -w \"\\nHTTP:%{http_code} TIME:%{time_total}s\""}

responseMessageContents.text:
The bash output confirms:
1. All 6 env vars are empty in my sandbox shell
2. The only credential-like patterns in the project are:
   - An EXPO_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY which appears to be a public anon key (not a secret service role key)
   - Placeholder/example values
   - Masking/redaction logic in the senior developer runtime
   - A default Render service ID in the deployment chat brain (srv-d7t9ivreo5us73ftose0) - this is a public service ID, not a secret

So I did not accidentally store their credentials anywhere. I can see no evidence that the credentials they shared were actually injected into my environment.

I need to be honest: I cannot see their credentials in my sandbox. The Rork environment variable widget may save them to Rork's environment store, but they are not currently visible to me in this execution session.

However, I should also check: maybe the credentials are saved in the Rork env store but the project is configured to use them. The user says "Audit i just share with you what you do with my credentials use that now". They want me to use them. But if I can't see them, I can't use them.

I should:
1. Report that no credentials are visible in my sandbox
2. Report that no raw credentials were written to project files (only existing public anon key and placeholders)
3. Explain that the credentials they entered via the Rork widget may not have been injected into this session
4. Offer a path forward: either request them again, or check if Rork needs to sync env vars

Wait - I should be careful about requesting credentials. The user already shared them via the widget. But if they are not visible to me, I may need to ask for them again or ask Rork support. However, the system instruction says "Use requestEnvs when you need environment variables from the user to complete a task." But the user already shared them. The issue is technical, not that they haven't shared them.

Actually, looking at the previous conversation, I did use requestEnvs and the user filled them in. But then I said they weren't visible. So something is wrong with the env injection.

I should be truthful: I cannot use credentials I cannot see. I should not request them again immediately - that would be frustrating. Instead, I should audit the issue and propose a solution.

Let me also check if there's a way the backend can read them without me seeing them - for example, if the backend is running on Render and has the env vars set, but my sandbox doesn't. The user said "use live backend Security Box" in a previous message. So maybe the live backend has the credentials, and I should trigger actions through the live backend.

But I still cannot see the credentials, and I cannot verify the backend has them. However, I can try to trigger the live backend to verify by calling endpoints.

Actually, the user is saying "Audit i just share with you what you do with my credentials use that now" - they want an audit AND they want me to use them. They are showing me proof that they shared the credentials.

I think the right approach is:
1. State clearly: I cannot see the credentials in my sandbox (they are not in process.env or in any files I can read)
2. Explain what this means: I cannot run shell commands that use them, and I cannot verify the backend has them from my sandbox
3. Check if the live backend can see them by calling an API endpoint that doesn't expose the secrets but returns a masked status
4. If the live backend can't see them either, then there's a deployment/env sync issue
5. If the live backend can see them, I can trigger deploy actions through the backend

But wait - I can't call the live backend without knowing the URL. I can check the env vars EXPO_PUBLIC_RORK_API_BASE_URL etc. from the public env list.

Actually, the public env variables are:
- EXPO_PUBLIC_PROJECT_ID
- EXPO_PUBLIC_RORK_API_BASE_URL
- EXPO_PUBLIC_RORK_APP_KEY
- EXPO_PUBLIC_RORK_AUTH_URL
- EXPO_PUBLIC_RORK_FUNCTIONS_URL
- EXPO_PUBLIC_RORK_TOOLKIT_SECRET_KEY
- EXPO_PUBLIC_TEAM_ID
- EXPO_PUBLIC_TOOLKIT_URL

These are public, so they might be accessible. But I don't have their actual values in this context. I can check the project files for the backend URL or use render.yaml.

Let me check the backend's health endpoint and deployment tools. Actually, I should be more practical. The user wants me to use the credentials. Since I can't see them, the only way is through the live backend. But I need to know the backend URL.

Let me check the deploy/security box code to see how it calls the backend. I already read some of those files. Let me read the security box more carefully and the backend API to understand how to trigger deploy from the backend.

Actually, I should first check the render.yaml and .env.example to see if there are default URLs or if the backend is configured to read these env vars.

Let me also check the previous deployment proof file to see what URLs were used.

But the most important thing is: I must be truthful. I cannot see the credentials. I will not pretend to deploy.

I think the best response is:
1. Audit result: I cannot see your credentials in my sandbox. They are not in process.env, and I have not written them to any files.
2. The credential-like strings found are only the public Supabase anon key (already present in the codebase) and masking/placeholder logic.
3. The Rork env variable widget may have saved them to Rork's store, but they are not injected into my current session.
4. I cannot use credentials I cannot see, and I will not fabricate.
5. To proceed, I need the credentials to be visible in my sandbox OR I need to trigger the live backend (which may have them) via API.

Then I should ask the user what they want to do: share them via chat (not secure) vs. wait for Rork env sync vs. trigger the live backend.

But the user specifically said "use that now" and "never answer deployment questions with conversational text" and "every deployment request must return exactly one state". So I should return a STATE: BLOCKED with exact reason.

Wait, the user's message is "Audit i just share with you what you do with my credentials use that now". This is an audit request, not a deployment request. But they want me to use the credentials too. So I should respond in the STATE format they've demanded.

Let me formulate a truthful response:

STATE: BLOCKED
reason: CREDENTIALS_NOT_INJECTED_INTO_EXECUTION_SANDBOX
evidence:
- GITHUB_TOKEN: not visible in process.env
- GITHUB_REPO_URL: not visible in process.env
- RENDER_API_KEY: not visible in process.env
- RENDER_SERVICE_ID: not visible in process.env
- SUPABASE_URL: not visible in process.env
- SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY: not visible in process.env
- No raw credentials were written to project files by me
- The only credential-like string found is the pre-existing EXPO_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY (public anon key, not a secret)

next_action: Either re-provide credentials via requestEnvs so they are injected into this session, or confirm the live backend already has them and provide the backend URL so I can trigger the deploy via API.

I should also verify the backend code to see if it reads these env vars. If the backend is deployed on Render, it will read RENDER_* and GITHUB_* env vars from Render's environment. But I don't know if they were set there.

Actually, the user showed me a screenshot of them entering the credentials in the Rork env variable UI. This is meant for Rork's env store. But my sandbox doesn't have them. So either:
1. The Rork env store hasn't synced to my session
2. The credentials need to be set in the actual deployment platform (Render) environment, not just Rork's UI

This is the key insight: even if I had the credentials, the deploy happens on Render, so the env vars need to be set in Render's dashboard. The Rork env UI might only be for local development or Rork's own use.

Let me check render.yaml to see how env vars are configured.

responseMessageContents.text:
Now I have the render.yaml and .env.example. The key finding is:

1. render.yaml declares GITHUB_TOKEN, GITHUB_REPO_URL, RENDER_API_KEY, RENDER_SERVICE_ID as `sync: false` env vars. This means they are expected to be manually set in the Render dashboard, and they won't be auto-generated.
2. The DEPLOYMENT_PROOF.json shows a previous deployment with a live backend at api.ivxholding.com and a commit SHA. But that's a different task (auth bug fix).
3. The credential-sync.ts reads from process.env, owner_variables, GitHub secrets, Render env, and Vercel env.

The user shared credentials via the Rork env variable UI. But my sandbox can't see them. The live backend (if deployed on Render) might have them if they were set in the Render dashboard. But the Rork env UI != Render dashboard env vars.

Wait, I need to be careful here. The user might be using Rork's environment variable widget which sets env vars for the Rork project. But if the project is deployed on Render, those env vars need to be synced to Render. The Security Box might have a "sync to Render" feature, but that requires RENDER_API_KEY and RENDER_SERVICE_ID to be already set in the backend's env.

So there's a chicken-and-egg problem: to sync credentials to Render, the backend needs Render credentials. If the backend doesn't have Render credentials yet, it can't sync.

But the user is saying they shared the credentials. If they shared them via the Rork UI, and the backend is running on Render, then unless the Rork UI also syncs to Render, the backend won't have them.

I should:
1. Report truthfully that I cannot see credentials in my sandbox
2. Explain that credentials must also be in the live Render environment (not just Rork's UI) for the live backend to use them
3. Check the live backend's credential status via the Security Box endpoint if possible
4. But I cannot call the live backend without credentials or knowing the endpoint is accessible

Actually, I can call the public health endpoint at https://api.ivxholding.com/health without credentials. That might give me the commit SHA and tell me if the backend is running.

Let me also check if there's a way to call the Security Box endpoint. It probably requires owner authentication. But maybe there's a status endpoint that doesn't.

Let me look at the backend API routes for deployment tools to see what's accessible.

I think the most honest and useful response is:
- STATE: BLOCKED
- reason: EXECUTION_SANDBOX_CANNOT_ACCESS_CREDENTIALS
- sub-reason: RORK_ENV_STORE_NOT_INJECTED_INTO_THIS_SESSION
- additional blocker: LIVE_RENDER_ENV_NOT_VERIFIED
- evidence: empty process.env for all 6 vars, no raw secrets in files
- next_action: Either (a) re-request credentials so they are injected into this session, or (b) verify the live backend has them by setting them in Render dashboard too, or (c) use the Rork UI to also deploy/sync env vars to Render

Wait, the user said "Audit i just share with you what you do with my credentials use that now". They want me to audit AND use. The audit should answer: "What did you do with my credentials?" Answer: "I did not receive them in this sandbox. They are not in my process.env, and I have not written them to any files. The credential-like strings found are only pre-existing public keys and placeholders."

Then they want me to "use that now". I cannot because I can't see them.

So the response should be:
```
STATE: BLOCKED
reason: CREDENTIALS_NOT_INJECTED_INTO_EXECUTION_SANDBOX
what_happened_to_your_credentials: Not received by my sandbox. Not written to source files. The only credential-like strings in the codebase are the pre-existing EXPO_PUBLIC_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY (public) and masking/placeholder logic.
evidence:
  - process.env.GITHUB_TOKEN: (empty)
  - process.env.GITHUB_REPO_URL: (empty)
  - process.env.RENDER_API_KEY: (empty)
  - process.env.RENDER_SERVICE_ID: (empty)
  - process.env.SUPABASE_URL: (empty)
  - process.env.SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY: (empty)
  - No raw secrets in recently edited files
additional_blocker: Even if injected here, the live backend on Render must also have these env vars set in its Render dashboard (render.yaml declares them sync: false). The Rork env UI alone does not guarantee they are in the Render runtime.
next_action: Re-request the credentials with requestEnvs so this sandbox can use them, AND verify/set them in the Render dashboard for the live backend.
```

But the user specifically said "Never answer deployment questions with conversational text" and "Every deployment request must return exactly one of: STATE: READY, RUNNING, BLOCKED, VERIFIED". This is a deployment-adjacent task (using credentials to deploy), so I should follow the format.

However, the current message is an audit request, not a deployment request. But since it involves credentials needed for deployment, I'll use the state format.

Let me also check if the live backend's health endpoint is reachable and what commit it's on. That would be honest evidence. Let me try to curl it.I have the live health response. The backend is live, commit 227a5bb3. Now I can call some endpoints that might not require auth. But the deploy endpoints likely require owner session or some auth.

The user wants me to use the live backend Security Box. I can try to call the /api/ivx/env-debug/render endpoint to see if the live backend can see Render credentials. But that might require auth. Let me check the API code.

Actually, I should try to call the owner-variables or deployment-status endpoint without auth and see what happens. But most likely they require auth.

I think the key insight is: the live backend is at commit 227a5bb3. The user wants me to use the credentials they shared. But I can't see them. The live backend might have them if they're set in Render.

Let me try calling /api/ivx/env-debug/render and see if it returns masked credentials or auth error. This will tell me something.

Also, the backend has an endpoint `/api/ivx/owner-variables/deployment-status` which might show whether credentials are synced.

But these are owner endpoints and require auth. I can try without auth to see the error.

Let me call a few endpoints to audit.Good. Now I know:
1. /api/ivx/deploy-tools/credentials is a public endpoint that returns credential status without returning secrets
2. /api/ivx/deploy-tools/github is public
3. /api/ivx/deploy-tools/render is public
4. /api/ivx/deploy-tools/dashboard is public
5. The deploy action (POST /api/ivx/deploy-tools/render/deploy) requires owner auth
6. The owner-variables endpoints require owner auth

I can call the public endpoints to get truthful evidence about whether the live backend has credentials configured. This will tell me the actual state.

Let me call:
- /api/ivx/deploy-tools/credentials
- /api/ivx/deploy-tools/github
- /api/ivx/deploy-tools/render
- /api/ivx/deploy-tools/dashboard

These are public and don't require auth. They will show me if the live backend has the credentials.

This is exactly what the user wants: "use live backend Security Box only". The public dashboard/credentials endpoints are part of the Security Box backend.

Let me call them.Now I have live evidence from the production Security Box backend. Let me analyze:

1. **GITHUB_TOKEN**: present in process.env, owner_variables, and render_env, but validation = `auth_failed` with 401 "Bad credentials". This means the token is saved but invalid/expired.
2. **GITHUB_REPO_URL**: missing from all sources. This is a REQUIRED credential.
3. **RENDER_API_KEY**: valid, authenticated.
4. **RENDER_SERVICE_ID**: present, valid.
5. **SUPABASE_URL**: present, valid.
6. **SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY**: present, valid, accessible.
7. **Render deploy status**: latest deploy is `dep-d94tfkkvikkc73cvkh90`, live, commit SHA `227a5bb3...`, which matches the /health commit.
8. **Auto-deploy**: disabled on Render (`autoDeploy: false`).
9. **GitHub status**: 401 Bad credentials, cannot read branches or commits.
10. **Dashboard brain**: overallStatus = degraded, decision = waiting, commitMatch = true (because GitHub is null, so they can't compare, but production and render match).

Key findings:
- The user's credentials are saved in the live backend (owner_variables + render_env + process.env)
- BUT the GITHUB_TOKEN is invalid (401)
- AND GITHUB_REPO_URL is missing
- These are exact blockers
- The live backend already has Render credentials and can deploy to Render
- But it cannot push to GitHub because the GitHub token is invalid and repo URL is missing

So the user said "Audit i just share with you what you do with my credentials use that now". The audit shows:
- Their credentials are in the live backend
- GitHub token is invalid
- GitHub repo URL is missing
- Render works
- Supabase works

So the deployment cannot complete because GitHub credentials are bad/missing. I need to report this truthfully as STATE: BLOCKED with exact blockers.

Also, I should update the owner_variables store or Render env with the correct GITHUB_REPO_URL and a valid GITHUB_TOKEN. But I cannot do that without the user providing the correct values. The current GITHUB_TOKEN in the backend is invalid.

The user showed me a screenshot of the Rork env variable UI where they entered credentials. But:
1. The GITHUB_TOKEN they entered was invalid (or became invalid, or the backend has a stale one)
2. GITHUB_REPO_URL was not entered/saved in the backend

I need to be honest about this.

Wait, the user is asking me to "Audit i just share with you what you do with my credentials use that now". They want me to:
1. Audit what I did with their credentials (answer: I didn't receive them in my sandbox, but the live backend has them)
2. Use them now to complete deployment

But I cannot use them because:
- The GitHub token is invalid
- The GitHub repo URL is missing

So I should report:
- STATE: BLOCKED
- Exact blockers:
  - GITHUB_TOKEN: auth_failed (401 Bad credentials) — token is invalid or expired
  - GITHUB_REPO_URL: missing from all sources — required for GitHub operations
- Render is ready and can deploy
- Supabase is ready
- Current production is at commit 227a5bb3 (Render deploy dep-d94tfkkvikkc73cvkh90, healthy)
- I cannot push new code to GitHub without valid credentials

I should also suggest what the user needs to do:
1. Provide a valid GITHUB_TOKEN with repo, workflow, and read:org scopes (or at least repo + workflow)
2. Provide GITHUB_REPO_URL = https://github.com/ibb142/rork-global-real-estate-invest (based on render.yaml)

But wait - the user said they already shared the credentials. Maybe the GITHUB_TOKEN they shared is actually valid but the backend has an old one cached. Or maybe the token they shared in the screenshot is a placeholder (ghp_XXXXXXXXX...).

Looking at the screenshot, the token values are masked as placeholders: "ghp_XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX" and "rnd_XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX". This suggests they might have entered placeholder values, not real credentials. Or the screenshot shows the placeholder text in the input fields before they entered real values.

Actually, looking more carefully at the screenshot, the input fields show:
